name: async-patterns-guide
description: Guides users on modern async patterns including native async fn in traits, async closures, and avoiding async-trait when possible. Activates when users work with async code.
capabilities: Identifies async-trait usage and evaluates if native async fn can replace it
Explains when to use static vs dynamic dispatch for async traits
Provides migration examples from async-trait to native patterns
Analyzes async code for performance bottlenecks and optimization opportunities
author: EmilLindfors
securitySummary: Static analysis detected 18 patterns but manual review confirms all are false positives. The skill contains only documentation with Rust code examples. The 'external_commands' findings are Markdown code fences misidentified as shell backticks. 'Sensitive' findings reference generic database variables, not SQLite. 'Weak cryptographic algorithm' and 'System reconnaissance' findings are unrelated pattern matches in the description text. No executable code exists in this skill.
valueStatement: Rust developers often use outdated async patterns that add unnecessary dependencies and performance overhead. This skill provides expert guidance on native async fn in traits, async closures, and helps migrate from async-trait to cleaner, faster code.
limitations: Does not modify or write code files directly - only provides guidance and examples
Does not run or test async code for correctness
Does not handle platform-specific async runtimes (tokio, async-std)

useCases.targetUser: Rust developers working on production async codebases
useCases.title: Migrate from async-trait to Native Async
useCases.description: A developer wants to simplify their codebase by removing the async-trait dependency. The skill evaluates their code and provides a migration path to native async fn in traits with zero runtime overhead.

useCases.targetUser: Rust developers new to async programming
useCases.title: Learn Modern Async Patterns
useCases.description: A Rust developer learning async programming wants to understand best practices from the start. The skill explains when to use native async fn, async closures, and helps avoid common mistakes.

useCases.title: Optimize Async Performance
useCases.description: A developer notices performance issues in async code and suspects dispatch overhead. The skill analyzes whether static or dynamic dispatch is appropriate and suggests architectural improvements.

promptTemplates.title: Basic Pattern Check
promptTemplates.prompt: I'm using async-trait in my Rust project. Can you review my code and suggest if I can switch to native async fn in traits?
promptTemplates.scenario: User wants to evaluate migration from async-trait

promptTemplates.title: Migration Guidance
Async Closure Usage
promptTemplates.prompt: How do I use async closures to process a list of items concurrently in Rust 1.85+? Show me a pattern with proper error handling.
promptTemplates.scenario: User wants to learn async closure patterns

promptTemplates.title: Performance Analysis
promptTemplates.prompt: I have an async function that takes Box<dyn MyTrait>. The performance seems slow. Can you explain the overhead and suggest alternatives for better performance?
promptTemplates.scenario: User wants to optimize async dispatch

bestPractices: Prefer native async fn in traits over async-trait when using Rust 1.75+ and static dispatch is acceptable
Use generics with trait bounds instead of Box<dyn Trait> to eliminate boxing overhead
Reserve async-trait for cases requiring true dynamic dispatch or object safety
Consider MSRV requirements before migrating from async-trait to native patterns
antiPatterns: Using Box<dyn Trait> with async-trait when static dispatch would suffice
Mixing async-trait patterns with generic code that forces monomorphization
Ignoring the performance impact of vtable lookups in hot async paths
Using async-trait without evaluating whether native async fn meets requirements

faq.question: What is async-trait and why would I want to avoid it?
faq.answer: async-trait is a crate that enables async fn in traits for Rust versions before 1.75. It uses macros and boxing under the hood, adding runtime overhead. Modern Rust (1.75+) supports native async fn in traits without these drawbacks.

faq.question: Does async-trait work with Rust 1.75+?
faq.answer: Yes, async-trait still works in Rust 1.75+, but native async fn is now preferred. You can use async-trait for dynamic dispatch or when MSRV requires older Rust versions.

faq.question: What is the performance difference between async-trait and native async fn?
faq.answer: async-trait uses Box<dyn Trait> for dynamic dispatch, adding vtable lookups and heap allocation. Native async fn with static dispatch eliminates these, providing zero-cost abstractions and better optimization.

faq.question: Can I mix async-trait and native async fn in the same project?
faq.answer: Yes, traits using async-trait and native async fn can coexist. However, using Box<dyn Trait> for async-trait traits and generics for native async traits creates different dispatch mechanisms.

faq.question: What are async closures and when should I use them?
faq.answer: Async closures (Rust 1.85+) are closures that return futures. They enable passing async logic as arguments without manually constructing future types. Useful for callbacks and higher-order async operations.

faq.question: How do I decide between static and dynamic dispatch for async traits?
faq.answer: Choose static dispatch (generics) when you know all trait implementations at compile time and want best performance. Choose dynamic dispatch (dyn Trait) when implementing plugins, storing heterogenous trait objects, or when compile-time type knowledge is impossible.
